explanation_box.text: Seems like a fairly typical looking, lizard-like creature that does not have a very high or very low fertility rate, just around average.
It has a charming look which attracted plenty of mates.
The pointed toes allowed them to dig into the dirt to lay eggs successfully, but there will always be a certain amount of eggs that don't make it.
The rounded toes made it so the reptile would not accidentally damage the eggs.
The pointed toes accidentally killed many of the eggs.
The bright color of the reptile made potential predators stay away from fear of poison, and maybe this reptile guarded the eggs.
The pointed toes damaged the eggs.
The rounded toes did not damage the eggs.
The rounded toes did not damage the eggs, and the warning-like color of the reptile scared predators off from the area.
The pointed toes damaged the eggs.
A perfectly average specimen, so as for eggs, naturally some will survive and some will not.
An average specimen with average fertility.

prompt_continuous: Reptiles varied in whether they were GREEN or RED. Did being GREEN or RED make a difference to fertility? If you did not notice this feature or believe it did not make a difference to fertility, please hit no and proceed.
question_text: Did color matter to the fertility of reptiles?

prompt_continuous: Reptiles varied in whether they had ROUND feet or POINTY, CURLY feet. Did having CURLY/POINTY or ROUND feet make a difference to fertility? If you did not notice this feature or believe it did not make a difference to fertility, please hit no and proceed.
question_text: Did foot shape matter to the fertility of reptiles?

feedback_text.text: It was really hard to remember the data enough to make a confident answer about which traits mattered to fertility. It was frustrating because the pictures went on for so long and I was running out of things to say.
